reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

/no_think 下面是一个 FNM unresolved cluster。请只返回 JSON 数组，不要解释。 本次只允许动作：match、ignore_ref、needs_review。 若截图已经能明确判断坏掉的数字或同页注释，请直接输出可自动落地的动作，不要退回 needs_review。 { "cluster_id": "toc-ch-005-psychobehavioralpatholog:toc-ch-005-psychobehavioralpatholog:endnote", "chapter_title": "Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine", "page_range": [ 46, 80 ], "note_system": "endnote", "request_mode": "anchor_rebind", "allowed_actions": [ "match", "ignore_ref", "needs_review" ], "page_contexts": [ { "page_no": 41, "ocr_excerpt": "task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity ..." }, { "page_no": 45, "ocr_excerpt": "The second half of this chapter traces how the growing prominence of mucus as a cause of illness starting in the eleventh century deeply altered the way in which Chinese physicians understood and handled madness. I first describe the origins of the notion of “mucus,” a pathological fluid that was not mentioned in the Inner Canon. I then review how the medical activism and publishing activities of the northern-Song government (976-1127) helped to create a pool of common references that facilitated medical debates and intertextual borrowings. For example, many Song doctors debated the claim made in a government-sponsored formulary that “Wind strokes” could be treated with vomit-inducing (“emetic”) drugs. Just around the same time (the turn of the twelfth century), these mucus-targeting emetics were starting to be used to treat madness. ### 3.1 The kuang 狂 core in the Yellow Emperor corpus Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was \"an unintegrated compilation of short writings\" (Sivin 1995f: 191). One will laugh recklessly and enjoy singing and making music, moving recklessly ..." }, { "page_no": 50, "ocr_excerpt": "Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE) is the earliest extant work proposing to treat psycho-behavioral symptoms with drug-based remedies. $ ^{13} $ It generally attributed fits of madness (發狂)—discussed in a section on Yang-brilliance disorders—to excessive Heat. Zhang Ji was probably describing the ability of high fever to create dementia. The symptoms were ghostly visions (also mentioned in Divine Pivot 22 and the Canon of the Pulse), wild language (妄語; Basic Questions 30), and ceaseless wild movements (妄行不休: Divine Pivot 22 and Canon of Problems 59). For fits of madness, the Treatise proposed to “make Blood descend” (下血); it prescribed “Decoction of resistance” (抵當湯) to treat “as if manic” cases (如狂, less severe than fits). For serious disorders of the Yang-brilliance vessel, with madness or possession symptoms, the “Great Qi-supporting decoction” (大承氣湯) was recommended. These recipes were all purgatives designed to expel the inner Heat that was thought to cause the symptoms. The Great Qi-supporting decoction later became an important remedy for madness (see 4.1, 4.2). Until the nineteenth century, physicians described the core symptoms of kuang with language borrowed ..." }, { "page_no": 51, "ocr_excerpt": "I noted in Chapter 1 that Martha Li Chiu (1986) scoured the Inner Canon to find a Chinese medical object comparable to mental illness, and that she settled on kuang because it was mostly characterized by \"mental symptoms.\" But by describing symptoms (rather than illnesses) as either mental or physical, she reproduced a problematic dichotomy at a lower level without uncovering the assumptions it hides. Below, I propose an alternative way of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatric ones. My goal is to foster our understanding of medical discourse on kuang by breaking free from the preconceptions that lurk beneath the notions of \"mental\" and \"physical.\" ### 3.2 Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Comparisons: why not “mental illness”? Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. unmatched_anchors.source_text: physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵

One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵

Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $

Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $

For the rest of imperial history, medical treatises described kuang with formulaic language derived from chapters 30 and 49 of the Basic Questions, which were not even explicitly concerned with kuang. Only one passage rivaled them in textual dominance—pian 22 (“Dian and kuang” 癲狂篇) of the Divine Pivot, which described “five mental syndromes [that] form a recognizable cluster around the focal concern of kuang” (Chiu 1986: 303). $ ^{9} $

When kuang starts to arise, one is initially dejected and tends to be forgetful, angry, and fearful. This originates in anguish or hunger.... When kuang begins to manifest itself, [one] rests little and one does not feel hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd and wise, or as being highly ranked. One is prone to scolding and yelling, never ceasing day or night.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] a tendency to be frightened and to laugh, a fondness for songs and music, and wild activity without cease originate in great fear.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] disorderly vision and hearing and a tendency to call out are produced by insufficient Qi.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] abundant eating, a tendency to see ghosts and spirits, and a tendency to laugh [to oneself] with no external display originate in having greatly rejoiced at something. $ ^{10} $

Descriptions of madness from the Inner Canon were soon copied into shorter texts, such as the Canon of Problems (Nanjing 難經; a second-century synthesis of the Inner Canon), Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses (Shanghan zabing lun 傷寒雜病論; ca. 200 CE), Huangfu Mi's 皇甫謐 AB Canon of Acumoxa (Zhenjiu jiayijing 針灸甲乙經; 256/262), and Wang Xi's 王熙 Canon of the Pulse (Maijing 脈經; third century).¹¹ The most commonly cited in later imperial times was the Canon of Problems.¹² "How are the illnesses kuang and dian to be distinguished?" (狂癲之病，何以別之), inquired the 59th problem.

Descriptions of madness from the Inner Canon were soon copied into shorter texts, such as the Canon of Problems (Nanjing 難經; a second-century synthesis of the Inner Canon), Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses (Shanghan zabing lun 傷寒雜病論; ca. 200 CE), Huangfu Mi's 皇甫謐 AB Canon of Acumoxa (Zhenjiu jiayijing 針灸甲乙經; 256/262), and Wang Xi's 王熙 Canon of the Pulse (Maijing 脈經; third century).¹¹ The most commonly cited in later imperial times was the Canon of Problems.¹² "How are the illnesses kuang and dian to be distinguished?" (狂癲之病，何以別之), inquired the 59th problem.

In several of his works, Liu called wuyun liuqi a “Mysterious Mechanism” (玄機), from a Taoist cosmological term that did not itself appear in the Inner Canon. $ ^{6} $ This notion

Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen in time" (Lo 2002: xxix).

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. A formal analysis would sh ...

The second half of this chapter traces how the growing prominence of mucus as a cause of illness starting in the eleventh century deeply altered the way in which Chinese physicians understood and handled madness. I first describe the origins of the notion of “mucus,” a pathological fluid that was not mentioned in the Inner Canon. I then review how the medical activism and publishing activities of the northern-Song government (976-1127) helped to create a pool of common references that facilitated medical debates and intertextual borrowings. For example, many Song doctors debated the claim made in a government-sponsored formulary that “Wind strokes” could be treated with vomit-inducing (“emetic”) drugs. Just around the same time (the turn of the twelfth century), these mucus-targeting emetics were starting to be used to treat madness. ### 3.1 The kuang 狂 core in the Yellow Emperor corpus Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen ...

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity. One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵ When this [vessel] is moved, one ails from: shivers and ailing from coldness; fondness for stretching; continual yawning, blackness in the center of the forehead; ailing from swelling; when severe, detestation for people and fire; when hearing the sound of wood, panicked alarm; panicky heart; the desire to close doors and windows and dwell in isolation; when the ailment is severe, the desire to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run. These constitute shin reversal. For these [ailments], the Yang-Brilliance vessel controls the treatment.

是勤〔動〕則病 洒洒 病塞＜寒＞ 喜信〔伸〕 數吹〔欠〕 嬿〔顏〕 墨 病種〔腫〕 至則惡人與火 聞木音則狄〔惕〕 然驚 心惕然欲獨必戶牖而處 病甚則欲乘高而歌 棄衣而走 此為骭蹶〔厥〕 是陽明脈主治 # Note the seamless transition between what we could call physical and behavioral symptoms. $ ^{6} $ # Two passages from the Basic Questions appear to have been comments on the above. Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $ “When the illness is serious, [one] discards clothes and runs about, mounts heights and sings. One may even not eat for several days. One will leap walls and ascend rooftops. One would not be able to climb [in such a way] ordinarily, yet when one is ill one unexpectedly can. Why is this?” Qibo said: “The four limbs are the root of all Yang [vessels]. This passage cast aside physical ills and replaced them with behavioral symptoms: refusal to eat, indiscriminate scolding, and climbing walls and ascending roofs.
For the rest of imperial history, medical treatises described kuang with formulaic language derived from chapters 30 and 49 of the Basic Questions, which were not even explicitly concerned with kuang. Only one passage rivaled them in textual dominance—pian 22 (“Dian and kuang” 癲狂篇) of the Divine Pivot, which described “five mental syndromes [that] form a recognizable cluster around the focal concern of kuang” (Chiu 1986: 303). $ ^{9} $ When kuang starts to arise, one is initially dejected and tends to be forgetful, angry, and fearful. This originates in anguish or hunger.... When kuang begins to manifest itself, [one] rests little and one does not feel hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd and wise, or as being highly ranked. One is prone to scolding and yelling, never ceasing day or night.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] a tendency to be frightened and to laugh, a fondness for songs and music, and wild activity without cease originate in great fear.... Descriptions of madness from the Inner Canon were soon copied into shorter texts, such as the Canon of Problems (Nanjing 難經; a second-century synthesis of the Inner Canon), Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses (Shanghan zabing lun 傷寒雜病論; ca. 200 CE), Huangfu Mi's 皇甫謐 AB Canon of Acumoxa (Zhenjiu jiayijing 針灸甲乙經; 256/262), and Wang Xi's 王熙 Canon of the Pulse (Maijing 脈經; third century).¹¹ The most commonly cited in later imperial times was the Canon of Problems.¹² "How are the illnesses kuang and dian to be distinguished?" (狂癲之病，何以別之), inquired the 59th problem. When kuang starts to manifest itself, one rarely rests and one does not feel hungry. One considers oneself to be elevated or of superior virtue, to be shrewd and wise, or to be of high rank, thus acting in a conceited manner. One will laugh recklessly and enjoy singing and making music, moving recklessly without cease. When dian starts to manifest itself, one's thoughts are unhappy. One stares straight and falls backward or forward. The Yin and Yang in all three sections of the vessel [where the puls ...

Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE) is the earliest extant work proposing to treat psycho-behavioral symptoms with drug-based remedies. $ ^{13} $ It generally attributed fits of madness (發狂)—discussed in a section on Yang-brilliance disorders—to excessive Heat. Zhang Ji was probably describing the ability of high fever to create dementia. The symptoms were ghostly visions (also mentioned in Divine Pivot 22 and the Canon of the Pulse), wild language (妄語; Basic Questions 30), and ceaseless wild movements (妄行不休: Divine Pivot 22 and Canon of Problems 59). For fits of madness, the Treatise proposed to “make Blood descend” (下血); it prescribed “Decoction of resistance” (抵當湯) to treat “as if manic” cases (如狂, less severe than fits). For serious disorders of the Yang-brilliance vessel, with madness or possession symptoms, the “Great Qi-supporting decoction” (大承氣湯) was recommended. These recipes were all purgatives designed to expel the inner Heat that was thought to cause the symptoms. The Great Qi-supporting decoction later became an important remedy for madness (see 4.1, 4.2). Until the nineteenth century, physicians described the core symptoms of kuang with language borrowed from the Inner Canon and its shorter derivative works, adopting in passing the notion that madness mostly meant unusual behavior. I am not claiming that the real symptoms displayed by people identifie ...

I noted in Chapter 1 that Martha Li Chiu (1986) scoured the Inner Canon to find a Chinese medical object comparable to mental illness, and that she settled on kuang because it was mostly characterized by "mental symptoms." But by describing symptoms (rather than illnesses) as either mental or physical, she reproduced a problematic dichotomy at a lower level without uncovering the assumptions it hides. Below, I propose an alternative way of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatric ones. My goal is to foster our understanding of medical discourse on kuang by breaking free from the preconceptions that lurk beneath the notions of "mental" and "physical." ### 3.2 Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Comparisons: why not “mental illness”? Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. From the era of “mad-doctoring” to the latest edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (or DSM; see APA 1994), behavioral symptoms have been at the center of psychiatric attention.
